title: MASLD / MASH
oneLiner: A metabolic liver disease spectrum from steatosis to steatohepatitis with fibrosis; driven by insulin resistance, obesity, and dyslipidaemia; leading cause of chronic liver disease globally, now surpassing viral hepatitis.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 52-year-old man with a BMI of 32, type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.5%) and hypertension on metformin and lisinopril attends a routine health check. He feels “a bit tired” but has no jaundice, pruritus, or abdominal pain. His liver enzymes show ALT 68 U/L, AST 52 U/L, GGT 95 U/L. Bilirubin, albumin, PT are normal. Abdominal ultrasound reveals a bright, echogenic liver consistent with steatosis. He drinks only 2–3 units of alcohol per week.

**Dissection of the vignette – pattern clues:**

- **Middle-aged / older adult** – prevalence peaks in 40s–60s; younger if severe genetic or metabolic syndrome.
- **Obesity (BMI >25–30)** – central driver; visceral adiposity releases free fatty acids and pro-inflammatory cytokines.
- **Type 2 diabetes** – insulin resistance → hyperinsulinemia → hepatic de novo lipogenesis.
- **Hypertension** – part of metabolic syndrome; often co-exists but not directly hepatotoxic.
- **Elevated ALT, AST (mild, <2x ULN)** – typical; AST/ALT <1 (unless advanced fibrosis → ratio may rise).
- **Elevated GGT out of proportion** – often highest in MASLD; reflects oxidative stress and biliary/metabolic dysfunction.
- **Normal synthetic function (albumin, PT)** – early disease; decompensation indicates cirrhosis.
- **Bright echogenic liver on US** – >30% steatosis; sensitivity ~85% but operator-dependent.
- **Minimal or no alcohol** – essential to differentiate from ALD. “Social drinker” is a red flag – quantify units.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **Cryptogenic cirrhosis** – a patient presents with ascites or variceal bleed, no prior liver enzyme elevations. Ultrasound may show shrunken, nodular liver; no steatosis visible (fat can disappear as fibrosis advances). The diagnosis is made retroactively by history of metabolic syndrome and excluding other causes.
2. **Acute decompensation in a patient with previously unknown NASH cirrhosis** – e.g., a type 2 diabetic on a new SGLT2 inhibitor who develops transaminitis >5x ULN. Not drug-induced hepatitis – it’s a “NASH flare” or superimposed injury. Check for sepsis, biliary obstruction, de novo viral hepatitis.
3. **Incidental finding of elevated ferritin** – patient thought to have haemochromatosis, but genotyping negative. MASLD frequently elevates ferritin as an acute phase reactant. Always confirm with transferrin saturation (<45% typical of MASLD).
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
MASLD (Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease) is the accumulation of fat in the liver (>5% by histology or imaging) in the absence of significant alcohol consumption. When the fat causes inflammation and hepatocyte damage (ballooning, necrosis) with or without fibrosis it is called MASH (Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis). It is effectively the hepatic manifestation of the metabolic syndrome.

**Why this matters clinically:**

- **Prevalence:** Affects ~25–30% of the global adult population; up to 60–70% in those with type 2 diabetes. It is now the leading cause of liver transplantation in several countries.
- **Progressive nature:** 10–20% of those with MASH will develop cirrhosis within 10 years; once cirrhotic, risk of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is 2–4% per year, even without cirrhosis.
- **Common pitfalls:**
  - Assuming “fatty liver is benign” – true only for simple steatosis; MASH carries significant risk.
  - Overlooking alcohol use – patients often underreport; AUDIT-C questionnaire helps.
  - Misinterpreting elevated ferritin – can lead to unnecessary venesection in haemochromatosis.
  - Not screening for fibrosis – NASH fibrosis predicts mortality; must use non-invasive tests (FIB-4, ELF, transient elastography).
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of how MASLD develops:**

1. **Dysregulated adiposity & insulin resistance** – Visceral adipocytes release excess free fatty acids (FFAs) into portal circulation. Insulin resistance in adipose tissue fails to suppress lipolysis → more FFAs. In the liver, hyperinsulinemia drives *de novo lipogenesis* (DNL) via SREBP-1c activation.
2. **Hepatic steatosis** – FFAs are esterified into triglycerides and stored in lipid droplets inside hepatocytes. If the capacity to export as VLDL is exceeded, fat accumulates. On ultrasound, fat scatters sound waves → “bright liver”.
3. **Lipotoxicity & oxidative stress** – Not all fat is equal. Saturated free fatty acids (e.g., palmitate) cause ER stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and production of reactive oxygen species (ROS). ROS damage cellular membranes and DNA, and activate inflammatory pathways (JNK, NF-κB).
4. **Inflammation & ballooning** – Hepatocyte injury releases DAMPs (danger-associated molecular patterns) that recruit macrophages/Kupffer cells. Activated Kupffer cells produce TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β → local inflammation (lobular inflammation on histology). Hepatocytes swell and lose their normal polygonal shape – **ballooning degeneration** – a key histologic feature of MASH.
5. **Fibrosis activation** – Stellate cells are quiescent, storing vitamin A. Inflammatory cytokines (TGF-β, TNF) and lipotoxicity activate them into myofibroblasts that deposit collagen. Progressive fibrosis → bridging fibrosis → cirrhosis.

**Mechanism → Sign mapping:**

| Pathophysiologic process | Clinical/Imaging sign | Why |
|--------------------------|------------------------|-----|
| Steatosis (fat accumulation) | Bright echogenic liver on US; hepatomegaly on palpation | Fat scatters ultrasound; liver enlarges due to fat storage |
| Insulin resistance + hyperinsulinemia | Acanthosis nigricans (in severe cases) | Insulin stimulates keratinocyte proliferation in flexures |
| Oxidative stress | Elevated GGT | GGT is induced by oxidative stress and glutathione turnover |
| Hepatocyte injury (ballooning) | Elevated ALT & AST | Leakage of intracellular enzymes |
| Fibrosis (progressive) | AST/ALT ratio >1; low platelets; FIB-4 high | In advanced fibrosis, AST rises due to impaired clearance; platelet consumption in cirrhosis |
| Portal hypertension (cirrhosis) | Splenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, ascites | Increased portal pressure → splanchnic vasodilation, splenogastric congestion, hypoalbuminaemia |

**Connection to pattern recognition:**  
The common vignette’s mild transaminitis reflects ongoing hepatocyte injury but preserved synthetic function. The elevated GGT hints at oxidative stress. The absence of alcohol is critical – the same histology can be caused by alcohol (ASH), but MASLD is metabolic. The AST/ALT ratio <1 suggests early disease; if it flips >1 over time, suspect fibrosis progression.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
- **Common cause: Insulin resistance + visceral obesity**  
  Stem clue: *“A diabetic, hypertensive patient with raised waist circumference and hypertriglyceridaemia.”*

- **Genetic factors**  
  - *PNPLA3 I148M variant* – most common genetic risk factor, especially in Hispanic populations.  
  - *TM6SF2 E167K* – protective against steatosis but paradoxically increases fibrotic risk due to defective VLDL export.  
  Stem clue: *“Family history of NASH cirrhosis; patient not obese.”*

- **Dietary**  
  - Fructose consumption (soda, sweetened beverages) → drives DNL and gout risk.  
  - High saturated fat intake.  
  Stem clue: *“History of high-fructose diet, non-alcoholic but overfed.”*

- **Medications** (drug-induced steatohepatitis)  
  - Methotrexate, tamoxifen, amiodarone, glucocorticoids, valproate, antiretroviral therapy (especially older NRTIs).  
  Stem clue: *“Patient on long-term methotrexate for psoriasis with elevated AST/ALT.”*

- **Metabolic disorders**  
  - Lipodystrophy (congenital or acquired) – lack of subcutaneous fat → severe insulin resistance.  
  - Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) – hyperandrogenism and insulin resistance.  
  - Hypothyroidism, hypopituitarism.  
  Stem clue: *“Young woman with oligomenorrhoea, hirsutism, and elevated liver enzymes.”*

- **Rapid weight loss** (unusual trigger)  
  - Starvation or bariatric surgery with excessive calorie deficit → mobilisation of FFAs → acute steatohepatitis.  
  Stem clue: *“Patient lost 10 kg in 2 months on a very low-calorie diet – now presents with right upper quadrant pain and ALT 300.”*
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**  
MASLD is a diagnosis of exclusion. Required: steatosis on imaging or histology + at least one metabolic risk factor (overweight/obesity, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, hypertension) + exclusion of significant alcohol (>30 g/day men, >20 g/day women) and other liver diseases (viral, autoimmune, haemochromatosis, Wilson).

**Scoring systems for fibrosis risk (non-invasive):**

- **FIB-4** = (Age x AST) / (Platelets x sqrt(ALT)).  
  Low risk: <1.3 (age <65) or <2.0 (age ≥65). High risk: >2.67. Intermediate: refer for elastography.  
- **NAFLD fibrosis score (NFS)** – uses age, BMI, impaired fasting glucose/diabetes, AST/ALT ratio, albumin, platelets.  
- **FibroScan (transient elastography)** – measures liver stiffness (kPa). <8 kPa: no significant fibrosis; 8–12: F2–F3; >12: probable F3–F4.

**Pattern recognition – what single finding confirms/rules out:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| LFTs (AST, ALT, GGT) | Hepatocyte injury + biliary/metabolic stress | First-line screen for liver disease | Mild elevation (1–2x ULN) typical; AST/ALT <1 in early, >1 in advanced fibrosis |
| Fasting glucose/HbA1c | Insulin resistance / diabetes | Risk stratification; diabetes increases MASH risk | HbA1c >6.5% indicates diabetes; even prediabetes (5.7–6.4%) relevant |
| Lipid profile (TG, HDL) | Hypertriglyceridaemia, low HDL | Metabolic syndrome component | TG >1.7 mmol/L, HDL <1.0 (men)/<1.3 (women) |
| Platelet count | Portal hypertension / cirrhosis | Screen for advanced fibrosis | <150 x10^9/L suggests cirrhosis |
| Ferritin + transferrin saturation | Iron overload vs acute phase | Exclude haemochromatosis | Ferritin elevated in MASLD (often 2–3x ULN); Tsat <45% argues against haemochromatosis |
| Anti-HCV, HBsAg, ANA, AMA, anti-LKM1 | Exclude other causes | Diagnostic requirement | Negative: supports MASLD |
| Ultrasound abdomen | Steatosis | First imaging; cheap, accessible | Bright liver, increased hepatorenal contrast; cannot differentiate simple steatosis from MASH |
| FibroScan (transient elastography) | Liver stiffness (fibrosis) | Non-invasive fibrosis assessment | CAP (controlled attenuation parameter) >275 dB/m indicates steatosis; stiffness >8 kPa suggests advanced fibrosis |

**Confirmatory test (rarely first-line now):**  
Liver biopsy – gold standard for steatohepatitis (ballooning, Mallory-Denk bodies, lobular inflammation, fibrosis stage). Only performed when non-invasive results are conflicting or to diagnose alternative causes (e.g., autoimmune hepatitis overlap).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – not applicable for most outpatients**  
No acute therapy unless decompensated liver disease (ascites, variceal bleed, encephalopathy). Manage those emergently per cirrhosis guidelines.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – for admitted patients with acute flare or worsening LFTs**  
- **Assess for precipitant:**  
  - Check for infection (SBP in cirrhotic patients), drug-induced injury (e.g., new statin, amiodarone).  
  - Withdraw any potential hepatotoxic drugs.  
- **Nutrition & lifestyle consult:**  
  - Dietitian referral for calorie deficit (500–1000 kcal/day) with macronutrient balance (low saturated fat, avoid fructose-sweetened beverages).  
  - Encourage physical activity (150 min/week moderate intensity).  
  - **Weight loss target:** ≥7% total body weight reduces steatosis and inflammation; ≥10% can regress fibrosis.  
- **Manage metabolic components:**  
  - Optimise glycaemic control. Use **pioglitazone** 30 mg once daily (if no heart failure) or **GLP-1 receptor agonists** (e.g., semaglutide 0.5–1 mg SC weekly) – both improve steatohepatitis in trials.  
  - **Vitamin E** 800 IU/day (α-tocopherol) – reduces inflammation in non-diabetic adults with biopsy-proven MASH. **Do not use in diabetics** – increased cardiovascular risk.  
  - **Statin** (atorvastatin 20–40 mg po once daily) – indicated for atherogenic dyslipidaemia; safe in MASLD (no risk of hepatotoxicity as previously feared).  
  - **Fibrate** (fenofibrate 145 mg po once daily) – for severe hypertriglyceridaemia only.  
- **Fibrosis assessment:**  
  - Calculate FIB-4; if >2.67 or intermediate with risk factors, arrange FibroScan.  
  - Refer to hepatology for advanced fibrosis (F3-F4) for HCC surveillance (alpha-fetoprotein + US every 6 months).

**Long-term**  
- **Weight reduction:**  
  - Bariatric surgery (sleeve gastrectomy or Roux-en-Y) – highly effective, can reverse MASH and fibrosis. Candidates: BMI >35 with metabolic comorbidity.  
  - Pharmacotherapy: **orlistat** 120 mg TID with meals – modest weight loss; alternative if surgery not suitable.  
- **Pharmacotherapy for MASH:**  
  - **Obeticholic acid** (OCA) 10–25 mg po once daily – FXR agonist, approved in some countries for MASH with fibrosis F2-F3. Side effects: pruritus (common), LDL increase.  
  - **Resmetirom** – thyroid hormone receptor β agonist; recently approved (USA) for MASH with moderate to advanced fibrosis. Dose 80 mg po once daily (based on weight). Assess response after 12 months.  
- **Liver transplantation** – for decompensated cirrhosis or HCC; MASH is now the second most common indication. Post-transplant recurrence rates >50% due to ongoing metabolic syndrome.

**Highlight what NOT to do (common pitfalls):**  
- **Don’t** stop statins because of mildly elevated LFTs – the cardiovascular benefit far outweighs the risk of statin-induced liver injury (very rare).  
- **Don’t** automatically start vitamin E in diabetic patients – increased haemorrhagic stroke and prostate cancer risk.  
- **Don’t** biopsy every patient with mild steatosis – use FIB-4/elastography to risk-stratify.  
- **Don’t** overlook alcohol – always quantify with AUDIT-C; even moderate alcohol >14 units/week can worsen fibrosis.  
- **Don’t** use ursodeoxycholic acid – no proven benefit in MASLD (useful only in primary biliary cholangitis).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | MASLD | Alcoholic liver disease (ALD) | Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) | Drug-induced liver injury (DILI) |
|---------|-------|-----------------------------|----------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Pain | Usually none or vague RUQ fullness | Epigastric/RUQ pain (acute alcoholic hepatitis: tender hepatomegaly) | Often none; may have fatigue, arthralgia | Jaundice, RUQ pain, pruritus |
| Exam | Acanthosis nigricans, central obesity, hepatomegaly | Palmar erythema, spider nevi, parotid enlargement (chronic) | Hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, extrahepatic (rash, arthritis) | Jaundice, rash, fever (hypersensitivity) |
| Labs typical | ALT > AST (early); AST/ALT <1.5; GGT high; ferritin elevated | AST > ALT (ratio >2); GGT very high (often >3x ALT); MCV high | ALT > AST; elevated IgG; positive ANA/ASMA/SLA; anti-LKM1 (type 2) | Variable; marked transaminitis or cholestatic; positive drug antibody tests (if immune-mediated) |
| Key distinguishing factor | Metabolic syndrome + minimal alcohol | Heavy drinking history (≥40 g/day) + positive CDT, PEth | Female preponderance, autoimmune features, positive autoantibodies | Temporal relation to drug (weeks–months); improvement on withdrawal |
| Diagnosis confirmation | Ultrasound steatosis + FIB-4/elastography; liver biopsy shows steatosis, ballooning, inflammation | History, AST/ALT >2, liver biopsy – steatosis, neutrophils, Mallory-Denk bodies | Liver biopsy – interface hepatitis, plasma cells; score (IAIHS) | Roussel Uclaf Causality Assessment Method (RUCAM) |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**If progressive MASH is missed or untreated:**

1. **Cirrhosis (compensated → decompensated)**  
   - Presents with fatigue, pruritus, bruising. Later: ascites, variceal haemorrhage (haematemesis, melena), hepatic encephalopathy (confusion, asterixis).  
   - Key signs: splenomegaly, thrombocytopenia <100 x10^9/L, prolonged PT, low albumin.  
   - What to do: refer for endoscopy (variceal screening), start NSBB (propranolol 20–40 mg BID for variceal prophylaxis), treat ascites with spironolactone 100 mg OD + furosemide 40 mg OD.  
2. **Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)**  
   - Can develop in non-cirrhotic MASH (10–20% of cases) – do not rely only on surveillance for cirrhotics.  
   - Presents: RUQ mass, weight loss, sudden decompensation. Alpha-fetoprotein may be normal (sensitivity ~60%).  
   - Imaging: US every 6 months; CT triple phase or MRI with contrast for diagnosis.  
   - Treatment: resection, TACE, ablation; sorafenib/lenvatinib for advanced stages.  
3. **Extrahepatic metabolic complications**  
   - Increased cardiovascular disease (MI, stroke) – MASLD is an independent risk factor beyond traditional metabolic syndrome.  
   - Chronic kidney disease – related to systemic lipotoxicity and inflammation.  
   - Type 2 diabetes progression – MASLD exacerbates insulin resistance.  
4. **Acute hepatic decompensation triggers**  
   - Infection (SBP), gastrointestinal bleed, electrolyte disturbance, non‑selective beta-blocker overuse, acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF).  
   - Stem clue: “A known NASH cirrhosis patient presents with confusion and low blood pressure – look for sepsis and hepatic encephalopathy.”

**Late presentation patterns (tested in exams):**  
- A 60-year-old diabetic man with incidental liver mass on US; no cirrhosis history; lesion is HCC. Think: MASH without cirrhosis.  
- A 45-year-old woman after “quick weight loss” bariatric surgery presents with jaundice and ALT 800. Think: Acute steatohepatitis from rapid fat mobilisation.  
- A patient with controlled blood pressure and normal LFTs yet with advanced fibrosis on non-invasive tests. Masquerading as normal – always consider FIB-4 in at-risk populations.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **M.A.S.H. Steps for management:**  
   - **M**etabolic control (glucose, lipids, BP)  
   - **A**dvise weight loss (≥7%), exercise  
   - **S**creen fibrosis (FIB-4, FibroScan)  
   - **H**epatology referral if F2–F4 / HCC surveillance  

2. **F.I.B.R.O.S.I.S.** for workup:  
   - **F**atty liver on US  
   - **I**nsulin resistance markers (waist, glucose, TG)  
   - **B**ile duct disease excluded (GTT)  
   - **R**are causes: haemochromatosis, Wilson, α1-AT  
   - **O**ut of alcohol? Check AUDIT-C  
   - **S**erology: viral hepatitis, autoantibodies  
   - **I**mage elastography  
   - **S**tiffness score high – biopsy if ambiguous  

**Clinical pearls:**

1. **AST/ALT ratio** – <1 suggests MASLD; >2 suggests Alcoholic liver disease. In advanced fibrosis, ratio flips >1. In acute viral hepatitis, ratio <1 again.
2. **Fatty liver on US does not equal MASH** – only 20% have significant inflammation/fibrosis. Use FIB-4 to risk-stratify.
3. **Normal LFTs do not rule out advanced fibrosis** – up to 30% of cirrhotic MASLD patients have normal ALT. Use FIB-4.
4. **Statins are safe and recommended** in MASLD – they improve cardiovascular outcomes and may reduce HCC risk. Do not discontinue for mild ALT elevation.
5. **Do not trust a patient’s “I only drink socially”** – use AUDIT-C. One positive answer (even 1–2 drinks per occasion) plus steatosis = evaluate further.
6. **Pioglitazone vs Vitamin E** – pioglitazone works in diabetics and non-diabetics; vitamin E only in non-diabetics. Both improve steatohepatitis but not fibrosis; weight gain with pioglitazone.
7. **Glucose-lowering drugs** – GLP-1 RAs (semaglutide) reduce hepatic steatosis and inflammation; SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin) may also help but less evidence. Avoid sulfonylureas if possible (weight gain).

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Calling fatty liver “benign” – always assess fibrosis risk.
- Confusing elevated ferritin with haemochromatosis – check Tsat and genotyping.
- Stopping statin because of mildly elevated LFTs – actually indicated.
- Forgetting to screen for HCC in non-cirrhotic MASH – persists as a blind spot.
- Over-sedating nausea/vomiting in cirrhosis – risk of precipitating encephalopathy.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior (Registrar/Consultant):**

- New diagnosis of decompensated cirrhosis (ascites, jaundice, variceal bleed, encephalopathy).
- Acute liver failure (INR >1.5 + any grade encephalopathy) – rare in MASLD unless drug-induced or acute-on-chronic.
- FIB-4 >3.25 (high risk) with FibroScan >12 kPa – need urgent hepatology referral.
- Rising liver enzymes >10x ULN (exclude DILI, viral hepatitis).
- Patient develops HCC – involve HPB team for curative options.

**When to escalate to critical care (ICU/MICU):**

- Variceal haemorrhage with haemodynamic instability (systolic BP <90, HR >120).
- Hepatic encephalopathy grade 3–4 (obtunded, unresponsive) with airway compromise.
- Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) – defined by organ failures (renal, respiratory, cardiovascular). CLIF-C ACLF score >60 predicts high mortality.
- Sepsis with multi-organ dysfunction (especially SBP with bacteraemia).
- Any patient with cirrhosis requiring vasopressors or mechanical ventilation.

**Specific thresholds:**

- **NEWS2** ≥7 (any single parameter of concern) – escalate.
- **Lactate** >4 mmol/L (sepsis/HO//hepatic hypoperfusion).
- **GCS** ≤12 (encephalopathy).
- **Platelets** <20 x10^9/L (awaiting cross-match for transfusion if variceal bleed).
- **INR** >2.5 (overt coagulopathy) – but use TEG/RoTEM to guide in bleeding states.
- **Creatinine** rising >50% from baseline or >1.5 mg/dL (>133 µmol/L) – hepatorenal syndrome.

*Every patient with MASLD cirrhosis should have an action plan: weight goal, medication list, surveillance schedule, and clear criteria for when to call the liver team.*

rawMarkdown:
## MASLD / MASH
**One-liner:** A metabolic liver disease spectrum from steatosis to steatohepatitis with fibrosis; driven by insulin resistance, obesity, and dyslipidaemia; leading cause of chronic liver disease globally, now surpassing viral hepatitis.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**
A 52-year-old man with a BMI of 32, type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.5%) and hypertension on metformin and lisinopril attends a routine health check. He feels “a bit tired” but has no jaundice, pruritus, or abdominal pain. His liver enzymes show ALT 68 U/L, AST 52 U/L, GGT 95 U/L. Bilirubin, albumin, PT are normal. Abdominal ultrasound reveals a bright, echogenic liver consistent with steatosis. He drinks only 2–3 units of alcohol per week.

**Dissection of the vignette – pattern clues:**

- **Middle-aged / older adult** – prevalence peaks in 40s–60s; younger if severe genetic or metabolic syndrome.
- **Obesity (BMI >25–30)** – central driver; visceral adiposity releases free fatty acids and pro-inflammatory cytokines.
- **Type 2 diabetes** – insulin resistance → hyperinsulinemia → hepatic de novo lipogenesis.
- **Hypertension** – part of metabolic syndrome; often co-exists but not directly hepatotoxic.
- **Elevated ALT, AST (mild, <2x ULN)** – typical; AST/ALT <1 (unless advanced fibrosis → ratio may rise).
- **Elevated GGT out of proportion** – often highest in MASLD; reflects oxidative stress and biliary/metabolic dysfunction.
- **Normal synthetic function (albumin, PT)** – early disease; decompensation indicates cirrhosis.
- **Bright echogenic liver on US** – >30% steatosis; sensitivity ~85% but operator-dependent.
- **Minimal or no alcohol** – essential to differentiate from ALD. “Social drinker” is a red flag – quantify units.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **Cryptogenic cirrhosis** – a patient presents with ascites or variceal bleed, no prior liver enzyme elevations. Ultrasound may show shrunken, nodular liver; no steatosis visible (fat can disappear as fibrosis advances). The diagnosis is made retroactively by history of metabolic syndrome and excluding other causes.
2. **Acute decompensation in a patient with previously unknown NASH cirrhosis** – e.g., a type 2 diabetic on a new SGLT2 inhibitor who develops transaminitis >5x ULN. Not drug-induced hepatitis – it’s a “NASH flare” or superimposed injury. Check for sepsis, biliary obstruction, de novo viral hepatitis.
3. **Incidental finding of elevated ferritin** – patient thought to have haemochromatosis, but genotyping negative. MASLD frequently elevates ferritin as an acute phase reactant. Always confirm with transferrin saturation (<45% typical of MASLD).

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
MASLD (Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease) is the accumulation of fat in the liver (>5% by histology or imaging) in the absence of significant alcohol consumption. When the fat causes inflammation and hepatocyte damage (ballooning, necrosis) with or without fibrosis it is called MASH (Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis). It is effectively the hepatic manifestation of the metabolic syndrome.

**Why this matters clinically:**

- **Prevalence:** Affects ~25–30% of the global adult population; up to 60–70% in those with type 2 diabetes. It is now the leading cause of liver transplantation in several countries.
- **Progressive nature:** 10–20% of those with MASH will develop cirrhosis within 10 years; once cirrhotic, risk of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is 2–4% per year, even without cirrhosis.
- **Common pitfalls:**
  - Assuming “fatty liver is benign” – true only for simple steatosis; MASH carries significant risk.
  - Overlooking alcohol use – patients often underreport; AUDIT-C questionnaire helps.
  - Misinterpreting elevated ferritin – can lead to unnecessary venesection in haemochromatosis.
  - Not screening for fibrosis – NASH fibrosis predicts mortality; must use non-invasive tests (FIB-4, ELF, transient elastography).

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story of how MASLD develops:**

1. **Dysregulated adiposity & insulin resistance** – Visceral adipocytes release excess free fatty acids (FFAs) into portal circulation. Insulin resistance in adipose tissue fails to suppress lipolysis → more FFAs. In the liver, hyperinsulinemia drives *de novo lipogenesis* (DNL) via SREBP-1c activation.
2. **Hepatic steatosis** – FFAs are esterified into triglycerides and stored in lipid droplets inside hepatocytes. If the capacity to export as VLDL is exceeded, fat accumulates. On ultrasound, fat scatters sound waves → “bright liver”.
3. **Lipotoxicity & oxidative stress** – Not all fat is equal. Saturated free fatty acids (e.g., palmitate) cause ER stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and production of reactive oxygen species (ROS). ROS damage cellular membranes and DNA, and activate inflammatory pathways (JNK, NF-κB).
4. **Inflammation & ballooning** – Hepatocyte injury releases DAMPs (danger-associated molecular patterns) that recruit macrophages/Kupffer cells. Activated Kupffer cells produce TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β → local inflammation (lobular inflammation on histology). Hepatocytes swell and lose their normal polygonal shape – **ballooning degeneration** – a key histologic feature of MASH.
5. **Fibrosis activation** – Stellate cells are quiescent, storing vitamin A. Inflammatory cytokines (TGF-β, TNF) and lipotoxicity activate them into myofibroblasts that deposit collagen. Progressive fibrosis → bridging fibrosis → cirrhosis.

**Mechanism → Sign mapping:**

| Pathophysiologic process | Clinical/Imaging sign | Why |
|--------------------------|------------------------|-----|
| Steatosis (fat accumulation) | Bright echogenic liver on US; hepatomegaly on palpation | Fat scatters ultrasound; liver enlarges due to fat storage |
| Insulin resistance + hyperinsulinemia | Acanthosis nigricans (in severe cases) | Insulin stimulates keratinocyte proliferation in flexures |
| Oxidative stress | Elevated GGT | GGT is induced by oxidative stress and glutathione turnover |
| Hepatocyte injury (ballooning) | Elevated ALT & AST | Leakage of intracellular enzymes |
| Fibrosis (progressive) | AST/ALT ratio >1; low platelets; FIB-4 high | In advanced fibrosis, AST rises due to impaired clearance; platelet consumption in cirrhosis |
| Portal hypertension (cirrhosis) | Splenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, ascites | Increased portal pressure → splanchnic vasodilation, splenogastric congestion, hypoalbuminaemia |

**Connection to pattern recognition:**  
The common vignette’s mild transaminitis reflects ongoing hepatocyte injury but preserved synthetic function. The elevated GGT hints at oxidative stress. The absence of alcohol is critical – the same histology can be caused by alcohol (ASH), but MASLD is metabolic. The AST/ALT ratio <1 suggests early disease; if it flips >1 over time, suspect fibrosis progression.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

- **Common cause: Insulin resistance + visceral obesity**  
  Stem clue: *“A diabetic, hypertensive patient with raised waist circumference and hypertriglyceridaemia.”*

- **Genetic factors**  
  - *PNPLA3 I148M variant* – most common genetic risk factor, especially in Hispanic populations.  
  - *TM6SF2 E167K* – protective against steatosis but paradoxically increases fibrotic risk due to defective VLDL export.  
  Stem clue: *“Family history of NASH cirrhosis; patient not obese.”*

- **Dietary**  
  - Fructose consumption (soda, sweetened beverages) → drives DNL and gout risk.  
  - High saturated fat intake.  
  Stem clue: *“History of high-fructose diet, non-alcoholic but overfed.”*

- **Medications** (drug-induced steatohepatitis)  
  - Methotrexate, tamoxifen, amiodarone, glucocorticoids, valproate, antiretroviral therapy (especially older NRTIs).  
  Stem clue: *“Patient on long-term methotrexate for psoriasis with elevated AST/ALT.”*

- **Metabolic disorders**  
  - Lipodystrophy (congenital or acquired) – lack of subcutaneous fat → severe insulin resistance.  
  - Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) – hyperandrogenism and insulin resistance.  
  - Hypothyroidism, hypopituitarism.  
  Stem clue: *“Young woman with oligomenorrhoea, hirsutism, and elevated liver enzymes.”*

- **Rapid weight loss** (unusual trigger)  
  - Starvation or bariatric surgery with excessive calorie deficit → mobilisation of FFAs → acute steatohepatitis.  
  Stem clue: *“Patient lost 10 kg in 2 months on a very low-calorie diet – now presents with right upper quadrant pain and ALT 300.”*

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**  
MASLD is a diagnosis of exclusion. Required: steatosis on imaging or histology + at least one metabolic risk factor (overweight/obesity, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, hypertension) + exclusion of significant alcohol (>30 g/day men, >20 g/day women) and other liver diseases (viral, autoimmune, haemochromatosis, Wilson).

**Scoring systems for fibrosis risk (non-invasive):**

- **FIB-4** = (Age x AST) / (Platelets x sqrt(ALT)).  
  Low risk: <1.3 (age <65) or <2.0 (age ≥65). High risk: >2.67. Intermediate: refer for elastography.  
- **NAFLD fibrosis score (NFS)** – uses age, BMI, impaired fasting glucose/diabetes, AST/ALT ratio, albumin, platelets.  
- **FibroScan (transient elastography)** – measures liver stiffness (kPa). <8 kPa: no significant fibrosis; 8–12: F2–F3; >12: probable F3–F4.

**Pattern recognition – what single finding confirms/rules out:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| LFTs (AST, ALT, GGT) | Hepatocyte injury + biliary/metabolic stress | First-line screen for liver disease | Mild elevation (1–2x ULN) typical; AST/ALT <1 in early, >1 in advanced fibrosis |
| Fasting glucose/HbA1c | Insulin resistance / diabetes | Risk stratification; diabetes increases MASH risk | HbA1c >6.5% indicates diabetes; even prediabetes (5.7–6.4%) relevant |
| Lipid profile (TG, HDL) | Hypertriglyceridaemia, low HDL | Metabolic syndrome component | TG >1.7 mmol/L, HDL <1.0 (men)/<1.3 (women) |
| Platelet count | Portal hypertension / cirrhosis | Screen for advanced fibrosis | <150 x10^9/L suggests cirrhosis |
| Ferritin + transferrin saturation | Iron overload vs acute phase | Exclude haemochromatosis | Ferritin elevated in MASLD (often 2–3x ULN); Tsat <45% argues against haemochromatosis |
| Anti-HCV, HBsAg, ANA, AMA, anti-LKM1 | Exclude other causes | Diagnostic requirement | Negative: supports MASLD |
| Ultrasound abdomen | Steatosis | First imaging; cheap, accessible | Bright liver, increased hepatorenal contrast; cannot differentiate simple steatosis from MASH |
| FibroScan (transient elastography) | Liver stiffness (fibrosis) | Non-invasive fibrosis assessment | CAP (controlled attenuation parameter) >275 dB/m indicates steatosis; stiffness >8 kPa suggests advanced fibrosis |

**Confirmatory test (rarely first-line now):**  
Liver biopsy – gold standard for steatohepatitis (ballooning, Mallory-Denk bodies, lobular inflammation, fibrosis stage). Only performed when non-invasive results are conflicting or to diagnose alternative causes (e.g., autoimmune hepatitis overlap).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – not applicable for most outpatients**  
No acute therapy unless decompensated liver disease (ascites, variceal bleed, encephalopathy). Manage those emergently per cirrhosis guidelines.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – for admitted patients with acute flare or worsening LFTs**  
- **Assess for precipitant:**  
  - Check for infection (SBP in cirrhotic patients), drug-induced injury (e.g., new statin, amiodarone).  
  - Withdraw any potential hepatotoxic drugs.  
- **Nutrition & lifestyle consult:**  
  - Dietitian referral for calorie deficit (500–1000 kcal/day) with macronutrient balance (low saturated fat, avoid fructose-sweetened beverages).  
  - Encourage physical activity (150 min/week moderate intensity).  
  - **Weight loss target:** ≥7% total body weight reduces steatosis and inflammation; ≥10% can regress fibrosis.  
- **Manage metabolic components:**  
  - Optimise glycaemic control. Use **pioglitazone** 30 mg once daily (if no heart failure) or **GLP-1 receptor agonists** (e.g., semaglutide 0.5–1 mg SC weekly) – both improve steatohepatitis in trials.  
  - **Vitamin E** 800 IU/day (α-tocopherol) – reduces inflammation in non-diabetic adults with biopsy-proven MASH. **Do not use in diabetics** – increased cardiovascular risk.  
  - **Statin** (atorvastatin 20–40 mg po once daily) – indicated for atherogenic dyslipidaemia; safe in MASLD (no risk of hepatotoxicity as previously feared).  
  - **Fibrate** (fenofibrate 145 mg po once daily) – for severe hypertriglyceridaemia only.  
- **Fibrosis assessment:**  
  - Calculate FIB-4; if >2.67 or intermediate with risk factors, arrange FibroScan.  
  - Refer to hepatology for advanced fibrosis (F3-F4) for HCC surveillance (alpha-fetoprotein + US every 6 months).

**Long-term**  
- **Weight reduction:**  
  - Bariatric surgery (sleeve gastrectomy or Roux-en-Y) – highly effective, can reverse MASH and fibrosis. Candidates: BMI >35 with metabolic comorbidity.  
  - Pharmacotherapy: **orlistat** 120 mg TID with meals – modest weight loss; alternative if surgery not suitable.  
- **Pharmacotherapy for MASH:**  
  - **Obeticholic acid** (OCA) 10–25 mg po once daily – FXR agonist, approved in some countries for MASH with fibrosis F2-F3. Side effects: pruritus (common), LDL increase.  
  - **Resmetirom** – thyroid hormone receptor β agonist; recently approved (USA) for MASH with moderate to advanced fibrosis. Dose 80 mg po once daily (based on weight). Assess response after 12 months.  
- **Liver transplantation** – for decompensated cirrhosis or HCC; MASH is now the second most common indication. Post-transplant recurrence rates >50% due to ongoing metabolic syndrome.

**Highlight what NOT to do (common pitfalls):**  
- **Don’t** stop statins because of mildly elevated LFTs – the cardiovascular benefit far outweighs the risk of statin-induced liver injury (very rare).  
- **Don’t** automatically start vitamin E in diabetic patients – increased haemorrhagic stroke and prostate cancer risk.  
- **Don’t** biopsy every patient with mild steatosis – use FIB-4/elastography to risk-stratify.  
- **Don’t** overlook alcohol – always quantify with AUDIT-C; even moderate alcohol >14 units/week can worsen fibrosis.  
- **Don’t** use ursodeoxycholic acid – no proven benefit in MASLD (useful only in primary biliary cholangitis).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | MASLD | Alcoholic liver disease (ALD) | Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) | Drug-induced liver injury (DILI) |
|---------|-------|-----------------------------|----------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Pain | Usually none or vague RUQ fullness | Epigastric/RUQ pain (acute alcoholic hepatitis: tender hepatomegaly) | Often none; may have fatigue, arthralgia | Jaundice, RUQ pain, pruritus |
| Exam | Acanthosis nigricans, central obesity, hepatomegaly | Palmar erythema, spider nevi, parotid enlargement (chronic) | Hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, extrahepatic (rash, arthritis) | Jaundice, rash, fever (hypersensitivity) |
| Labs typical | ALT > AST (early); AST/ALT <1.5; GGT high; ferritin elevated | AST > ALT (ratio >2); GGT very high (often >3x ALT); MCV high | ALT > AST; elevated IgG; positive ANA/ASMA/SLA; anti-LKM1 (type 2) | Variable; marked transaminitis or cholestatic; positive drug antibody tests (if immune-mediated) |
| Key distinguishing factor | Metabolic syndrome + minimal alcohol | Heavy drinking history (≥40 g/day) + positive CDT, PEth | Female preponderance, autoimmune features, positive autoantibodies | Temporal relation to drug (weeks–months); improvement on withdrawal |
| Diagnosis confirmation | Ultrasound steatosis + FIB-4/elastography; liver biopsy shows steatosis, ballooning, inflammation | History, AST/ALT >2, liver biopsy – steatosis, neutrophils, Mallory-Denk bodies | Liver biopsy – interface hepatitis, plasma cells; score (IAIHS) | Roussel Uclaf Causality Assessment Method (RUCAM) |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**If progressive MASH is missed or untreated:**

1. **Cirrhosis (compensated → decompensated)**  
   - Presents with fatigue, pruritus, bruising. Later: ascites, variceal haemorrhage (haematemesis, melena), hepatic encephalopathy (confusion, asterixis).  
   - Key signs: splenomegaly, thrombocytopenia <100 x10^9/L, prolonged PT, low albumin.  
   - What to do: refer for endoscopy (variceal screening), start NSBB (propranolol 20–40 mg BID for variceal prophylaxis), treat ascites with spironolactone 100 mg OD + furosemide 40 mg OD.  
2. **Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)**  
   - Can develop in non-cirrhotic MASH (10–20% of cases) – do not rely only on surveillance for cirrhotics.  
   - Presents: RUQ mass, weight loss, sudden decompensation. Alpha-fetoprotein may be normal (sensitivity ~60%).  
   - Imaging: US every 6 months; CT triple phase or MRI with contrast for diagnosis.  
   - Treatment: resection, TACE, ablation; sorafenib/lenvatinib for advanced stages.  
3. **Extrahepatic metabolic complications**  
   - Increased cardiovascular disease (MI, stroke) – MASLD is an independent risk factor beyond traditional metabolic syndrome.  
   - Chronic kidney disease – related to systemic lipotoxicity and inflammation.  
   - Type 2 diabetes progression – MASLD exacerbates insulin resistance.  
4. **Acute hepatic decompensation triggers**  
   - Infection (SBP), gastrointestinal bleed, electrolyte disturbance, non‑selective beta-blocker overuse, acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF).  
   - Stem clue: “A known NASH cirrhosis patient presents with confusion and low blood pressure – look for sepsis and hepatic encephalopathy.”

**Late presentation patterns (tested in exams):**  
- A 60-year-old diabetic man with incidental liver mass on US; no cirrhosis history; lesion is HCC. Think: MASH without cirrhosis.  
- A 45-year-old woman after “quick weight loss” bariatric surgery presents with jaundice and ALT 800. Think: Acute steatohepatitis from rapid fat mobilisation.  
- A patient with controlled blood pressure and normal LFTs yet with advanced fibrosis on non-invasive tests. Masquerading as normal – always consider FIB-4 in at-risk populations.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **M.A.S.H. Steps for management:**  
   - **M**etabolic control (glucose, lipids, BP)  
   - **A**dvise weight loss (≥7%), exercise  
   - **S**creen fibrosis (FIB-4, FibroScan)  
   - **H**epatology referral if F2–F4 / HCC surveillance  

2. **F.I.B.R.O.S.I.S.** for workup:  
   - **F**atty liver on US  
   - **I**nsulin resistance markers (waist, glucose, TG)  
   - **B**ile duct disease excluded (GTT)  
   - **R**are causes: haemochromatosis, Wilson, α1-AT  
   - **O**ut of alcohol? Check AUDIT-C  
   - **S**erology: viral hepatitis, autoantibodies  
   - **I**mage elastography  
   - **S**tiffness score high – biopsy if ambiguous  

**Clinical pearls:**

1. **AST/ALT ratio** – <1 suggests MASLD; >2 suggests Alcoholic liver disease. In advanced fibrosis, ratio flips >1. In acute viral hepatitis, ratio <1 again.
2. **Fatty liver on US does not equal MASH** – only 20% have significant inflammation/fibrosis. Use FIB-4 to risk-stratify.
3. **Normal LFTs do not rule out advanced fibrosis** – up to 30% of cirrhotic MASLD patients have normal ALT. Use FIB-4.
4. **Statins are safe and recommended** in MASLD – they improve cardiovascular outcomes and may reduce HCC risk. Do not discontinue for mild ALT elevation.
5. **Do not trust a patient’s “I only drink socially”** – use AUDIT-C. One positive answer (even 1–2 drinks per occasion) plus steatosis = evaluate further.
6. **Pioglitazone vs Vitamin E** – pioglitazone works in diabetics and non-diabetics; vitamin E only in non-diabetics. Both improve steatohepatitis but not fibrosis; weight gain with pioglitazone.
7. **Glucose-lowering drugs** – GLP-1 RAs (semaglutide) reduce hepatic steatosis and inflammation; SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin) may also help but less evidence. Avoid sulfonylureas if possible (weight gain).

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Calling fatty liver “benign” – always assess fibrosis risk.
- Confusing elevated ferritin with haemochromatosis – check Tsat and genotyping.
- Stopping statin because of mildly elevated LFTs – actually indicated.
- Forgetting to screen for HCC in non-cirrhotic MASH – persists as a blind spot.
- Over-sedating nausea/vomiting in cirrhosis – risk of precipitating encephalopathy.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior (Registrar/Consultant):**

- New diagnosis of decompensated cirrhosis (ascites, jaundice, variceal bleed, encephalopathy).
- Acute liver failure (INR >1.5 + any grade encephalopathy) – rare in MASLD unless drug-induced or acute-on-chronic.
- FIB-4 >3.25 (high risk) with FibroScan >12 kPa – need urgent hepatology referral.
- Rising liver enzymes >10x ULN (exclude DILI, viral hepatitis).
- Patient develops HCC – involve HPB team for curative options.

**When to escalate to critical care (ICU/MICU):**

- Variceal haemorrhage with haemodynamic instability (systolic BP <90, HR >120).
- Hepatic encephalopathy grade 3–4 (obtunded, unresponsive) with airway compromise.
- Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF) – defined by organ failures (renal, respiratory, cardiovascular). CLIF-C ACLF score >60 predicts high mortality.
- Sepsis with multi-organ dysfunction (especially SBP with bacteraemia).
- Any patient with cirrhosis requiring vasopressors or mechanical ventilation.

**Specific thresholds:**

- **NEWS2** ≥7 (any single parameter of concern) – escalate.
- **Lactate** >4 mmol/L (sepsis/HO//hepatic hypoperfusion).
- **GCS** ≤12 (encephalopathy).
- **Platelets** <20 x10^9/L (awaiting cross-match for transfusion if variceal bleed).
- **INR** >2.5 (overt coagulopathy) – but use TEG/RoTEM to guide in bleeding states.
- **Creatinine** rising >50% from baseline or >1.5 mg/dL (>133 µmol/L) – hepatorenal syndrome.

*Every patient with MASLD cirrhosis should have an action plan: weight goal, medication list, surveillance schedule, and clear criteria for when to call the liver team.*
